narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as an uphill battle, with an 84% chance of not passing. At the top of what shaped this view was your LSAT score of 18, the single strongest signal pulling toward failure, as it stands out as a tough hurdle in a field where that benchmark often predicts struggles ahead. Layered on that, your Year 3 decile of 1 made a big impression too, whispering to the model that sustaining strong performance through law school might be challenging based on patterns it knows. Your Year 1 decile of 1 echoed this, reinforcing a picture of early academic hurdles that tend to linger. Coming from a low family income bracket added another weight toward that tougher outlook, as the model sees how financial pressures can compound the odds without extra support. Your Asian background played a role here as well, tilting things further toward failure in the data it's learned from. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 nudged in that direction, painting a story of consistent rather than standout preparation. Your choice to go full-time tugged the prediction downward too, suggesting the intensity might overwhelm without balance. The one bright spot was your gender as male, which slightly pulled toward passing success in the model's view. All these pieces together—your LSAT leading the charge, backed by those low deciles and life circumstances—created a vivid narrative for the model of high risk, not a judgment on your worth, but a data-driven caution to protect you from potential heartbreak. This isn't the end of your story; it's a nudge to build up those core strengths like test prep or grades before reapplying. You've got resilience in you—use this to rewrite the next chapter. With empathy and belief in your potential, [Your Advisor].
